concept.headline: Coroutines are lightweight suspendable computations. suspend functions pause without blocking a thread. launch starts a coroutine. runBlocking bridges regular code and the coroutine world.
concept.explanation: A coroutine is like a very lightweight thread — it can be suspended (paused) and resumed later without occupying an OS thread. suspend marks a function that can pause execution. launch { } starts a new coroutine concurrently. runBlocking { } starts a coroutine scope and blocks the current thread until it finishes — used in main() and tests to bridge the blocking world. delay(ms) suspends the current coroutine without blocking the thread.
keyPoints: suspend fun — can be paused at suspension points (delay, await, etc.)
runBlocking { } — bridges blocking code to coroutines; blocks until all children finish
delay(ms) — non-blocking pause; suspends the coroutine, frees the thread

demo.intro: Watch how launch starts a concurrent coroutine and how the output order reveals coroutine scheduling.

explanation.what: runBlocking { } — creates a coroutine scope and blocks the main thread until done
explanation.why: main() is a regular blocking function; runBlocking bridges it into the coroutine world
explanation.change: a CoroutineScope is established; the lambda body runs as a coroutine
explanation.intent: Introduce runBlocking as the entry point for coroutines in main()
explanation.misconception: runBlocking DOES block the underlying thread. It is only acceptable in main() and tests. In production Android/server code you would use other builders (coroutineScope, viewModelScope, etc.)

explanation.what: launch { } — starts a new coroutine inside the scope
explanation.why: launch schedules the block for concurrent execution but returns immediately
explanation.change: a new coroutine is queued; execution continues at the next line in the parent
explanation.intent: Show launch as concurrent (non-blocking) coroutine start
explanation.misconception: launch does NOT wait for its block to finish — it is fire-and-forget. The parent coroutine keeps running immediately after launch {}.

explanation.what: println("Hello") runs immediately after launch returns
explanation.why: launch returns right away; the child coroutine has not started executing yet
explanation.intent: Show that the code after launch continues concurrently
explanation.misconception: Hello prints BEFORE World — even though launch { } appears earlier in the code. This surprises developers new to coroutines: the launched coroutine is scheduled but hasn't run yet when Hello prints.

explanation.what: delay(100L) — suspends the launched coroutine for 100 milliseconds
explanation.why: delay is a suspend function — it pauses the coroutine without blocking any thread
explanation.change: the launched coroutine is suspended; the thread is free to do other work
explanation.intent: Show non-blocking suspension — the key concept in coroutines
explanation.misconception: delay does NOT call Thread.sleep(). Thread.sleep blocks the thread. delay just registers a timer and frees the thread to run other coroutines.

explanation.what: After 100ms delay, the coroutine resumes and prints 'World'
explanation.why: delay completed — the coroutine is resumed from where it suspended
explanation.misconception: The coroutine resumes on the same dispatcher (thread pool). It does not need to restart from the beginning — it continues from the line after delay().

prediction.explanation: println("C") runs immediately after both launch calls return. A and B are scheduled but haven't run yet — they execute after C. So output is: C, then A, then B (in launch order).

explanation.afterDemo: Coroutines make concurrent code look sequential. delay looks like a blocking call but it is not — it suspends the coroutine and frees the thread. This is the power of structured concurrency: you write code top-to-bottom, the runtime handles the pausing and resuming. runBlocking is your entry point; launch is how you start concurrent work.
keyTakeaways: suspend fun can pause without blocking. delay(ms) is the simplest suspension.
launch { } starts a concurrent coroutine — returns immediately without waiting.
runBlocking { } is for main() and tests only — bridges blocking code to coroutines.

practice.prompt: Write a main that uses runBlocking to launch two coroutines: one prints "First" after delay(50L) and one prints "Second" after delay(100L). Also print "Start" before launching. Expected order: Start, First, Second.
practice.hint: Two launch blocks inside runBlocking. The shorter delay finishes first.

feedback.successMessage: Correct! Start prints immediately. First prints after 50ms, Second after 100ms. runBlocking waits for all children.
retryHints: launch { delay(50L); println("First") } — shorter delay fires first
runBlocking waits for both launched coroutines to complete before exiting

summary.title: Coroutines Basics — Complete
takeaways: Coroutines are lightweight — thousands can run on a few threads. suspend functions pause them without blocking.
launch { } starts a concurrent coroutine (fire-and-forget). Use await/join to wait for results.
runBlocking bridges main() to coroutines. In production, prefer structured scopes (viewModelScope, coroutineScope).
